b:T6177,<div class="specification">
<p>The graph shows the Bohr shift.</p>
<p style="text-align: center;"><img src="data:image/png;base64,"/></p>
<p style="text-align: center;">[Source: From <em>The New England Journal of Medicine</em>, Connie C.W. Hsia, Respiratory Function of<br/>Hemoglobin, Volume 338(4):239–47. Copyright © 1998, Massachusetts Medical Society. Reprinted with permission<br/>from Massachusetts Medical Society.]</p>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px; padding-right: 20px;">
<p>Using the graph, explain the Bohr shift.</p>
<div class="marks">[3]</div>
<div class="question_part_label">a.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px; padding-right: 20px;">
<p>State <strong>two</strong> modes of transport of carbon dioxide in blood. </p>
<p>1.</p>
<p>2. </p>
<div class="marks">[2]</div>
<div class="question_part_label">b.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px; padding-right: 20px;">
<p>State the effect of carbon dioxide in blood on the rate of ventilation.</p>
<div class="marks">[1]</div>
<div class="question_part_label">c.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px; padding-right: 20px;">
<p>Describe what happens to the hemoglobin from old or damaged red blood cells that are broken down.</p>
<div class="marks">[3]</div>
<div class="question_part_label">d.</div>
</div>c:T640,<div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p>a. respiring tissues produce CO<sub>2</sub>;</p>
<p>b. CO<sub>2</sub> leads to an increase in H<sup>+</sup>/decrease in blood pH;</p>
<p>c. increased acidity/decreased pH shifts the oxygen dissociation curve to the right;</p>
<p>d. affinity of the hemoglobin for oxygen is reduced;</p>
<p>e. greater release of oxygen from hemoglobin (at the same partial pressure of oxygen) in tissues;</p>
<div class="question_part_label">a.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p>a. dissolved/carried in plasma;</p>
<p>b. forms carbonic acid/H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub> (in plasma)<br/><em><strong>OR</strong></em><br/>as hydrogencarbonate (HCO<sub>3</sub>-) ions (in plasma);</p>
<p>c. binds to hemoglobin in red blood cells;</p>
<div class="question_part_label">b.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p>increased carbon dioxide in blood increases the rate of ventilation<br/><em><strong>OR</strong></em><br/>positive correlation/relationship;</p>
<div class="question_part_label">c.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p>a. hemoglobin (from broken red blood cells) taken up by Kupffer cells (in the liver);</p>
<p>b. hemoglobin broken down into heme and globin;</p>
<p>c. globin hydrolyzed/broken down to amino acids;</p>
<p>d. iron removed from heme group<br/><em><strong>OR</strong></em><br/>heme broken down to form bilirubin/bile pigment;</p>
<p><em>Do not accept red blood cells broken down into heme and globin</em>.</p>
<div class="question_part_label">d.</div>
</div>d:T4d5,<div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p>Most candidates did well in this question, but strongest candidates distinguished themselves with complete and very detailed answers. Although candidates did well, there were many inaccuracies in some answers. Bohr shift is the shift to the right of the oxygen dissociation curve in respiring tissues; it does not refer to the reverse process at lung level.</p>
<div class="question_part_label">a.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p>The majority of candidates did well also for this question, although some answers lacked accuracy.</p>
<div class="question_part_label">b.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p>Most candidates knew that CO<sub>2</sub> affects the rate of ventilation, but some did not specify that an increase in CO<sub>2</sub> caused an increase in ventilation rate, or vice versa.</p>
<div class="question_part_label">c.</div>
</div><div class="question" style="padding-left: 20px;">
<p>The majority of candidates described the process correctly; some stated incorrectly that red blood cells, instead of hemoglobin, are broken down into heme and globin.</p>
<div class="question_part_label">d.</div>
</div>2:["$","article",null,{"className":"q-page","children":[["$","p",null,{"style":{"margin":0},"children":["$","$L6",null,{"href":"/subject/biology/topic/d/","children":["← back to ","Biology"," topic ","d"]}]}],["$","h1",null,{"children":"20N.3.HL.TZ0.23"}],["$","div",null,{"className":"q-meta","children":[["$","span",null,{"className":"tag","children":"pestle"}],["$","span",null,{"children":"Biology"}],null,["$","span",null,{"children":"HL"}],["$","span",null,{"children":["Paper ","3"]}],["$","span",null,{"children":"20N"}],["$","span",null,{"children":["· ","d-6-transport-of-respiratory-gases"]}],["$","a",null,{"href":"https://pestle.pages.dev/app/","target":"_blank","rel":"noopener","children":"source ↗"}]]}],["$","div",null,{"className":"q-content","dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"$b"}}],["$","details",null,{"className":"solution","children":[["$","summary",null,{"children":"Markscheme / solution"}],["$","div",null,{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"$c"}}]]}],["$","details",null,{"className":"solution","children":[["$","summary",null,{"children":"Examiners’ report"}],["$","div",null,{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"$d"}}]]}]]}]
